question: What 4 documents must you bring to every flight?
answer_text: Student Pilot Certificate, Gov. Issued Document with Photo, Medical Certificate and Logbook with endorsements

question: Does a student pilot certificate expire?
answer_text: No

question: Medical certificate classes and privileges
answer_text: 1st: ATP, 2nd: Commercial, 3rd: PPL / Instructor

question: Medical duration under 40
answer_text: 1st class: 12 calendar months, 2nd class: 12 calendar months, 3rd class: 60 calendar months

question: Medical duration over 40
answer_text: 1st class: 6 calendar months, 2nd class: 12 calendar months, 3rd class: 24 calendar months

question: Student pilot solo privileges and limitations
answer_text: A student solo may go solo. May not carry passengers, carry anything for compensation or for hire. Must stay within 25nm of airport. May go to another airport only with additional endorsements. No touch & go’s. Must consult instructor prior to going solo.

question: How long is a solo endorsement valid?
answer_text: 90 days

question: Do you have any way of determining you are well enough to fly today?
answer_text: IMSAFE Illness, Medication, Stress, Alcohol, Fatigue, Emotion

question: Illness that commonly affects pilots
answer_text: Sinus infection

question: How do medications affect flying?
answer_text: All affect performance; check FAA/AME guidance

question: Alcohol rules (91.17)
answer_text: 8 hrs bottle-to-throttle, BAC < 0.04, no lingering effects

question: What is a METAR?
answer_text: Actual weather observation

question: METAR update frequency
answer_text: Hourly or when significant weather change

question: What is a TAF?
answer_text: Weather forecast

question: TAF validity
answer_text: 24 hours in 6-hour increments

question: What weather do you need to have to go solo?
answer_text: 1,000’ ceiling, 3 SM visibility VFR day and 5 SM for VFR night

question: What are the basic VFR weather minimums?
answer_text: [Image: VFR Airspace Weather Minimums Triangle]

question: Describe how to determine if the aircraft is airworthy
answer_text: Inspection: AV1ATES, Equipment: VFR Day – ATOMATOFLAMES, VFR Night - +FLAPS, Documents: SPARROW

question: SPARROW stands for
answer_text: It refers to the mandatory documents for a flight: Supplement avionics, Placards, Airworthiness Certificate, Registration, Radio operator license, Operating Limitations, Weight and Balance

question: AV1ATES stands for
answer_text: Annual inspection, VOR check, 100 hours inspection (commercial purposes), Airworthiness directives, Transponder check, ELT, Static/Altimeter/Pitot

question: Annual inspection interval
answer_text: 12 calendar months

question: VOR check interval
answer_text: 30 days

question: Transponder inspection
answer_text: 24 calendar months

question: Static system & Altimeter inspection
answer_text: 24 calendar months

question: ELT inspection
answer_text: 12 calendar months

question: ELT Battery
answer_text: 50% of useful life or 1 hour after cumulative use

question: Can an annual count as a 100-hour?
answer_text: Yes

question: Can a 100-hour count as an annual?
answer_text: No

question: What ATOMATOFLAMES is and what does it stand for?
What FLAPS is and what does it stand for?
answer_text: [Image: FLAPS required equipment mnemonic]

question: Aircraft registration duration
answer_text: Federal: 36 months, State: 24 months

question: Is a generic POH acceptable?
answer_text: No. It must be aircraft-specific

question: Forward CG characteristics
answer_text: Harder takeoff and landing, worse cruise, easier stall recovery

question: Aft CG characteristics
answer_text: Easier takeoff and landing, better cruise, worse stall recovery

question: Inoperative equipment regulation flow
answer_text: [Image: Inoperative equipment regulation flowchart]

question: What must be done with inoperative equipment?
answer_text: Remove/deactivate & label INOP

question: Takeoff performance determined from
answer_text: POH takeoff & landing charts

question: Crosswind limitations found in
answer_text: POH: 15 knots

question: Regulation for minimum safe altitudes
answer_text:
Anywhere – An altitude allowing, if a power unit fails, an emergency landing without hazard to any person or property.
Over congested areas – 1000 feet above highest obstacle within 2000 feet radius.
Other congested areas – 500 feet above surface. If over sparsely populated area or water, 500 feet above any person, vessel, vehicle or structure.

question: Regulation for right-of-way rules
answer_text:
General Priority: Aircraft in Distress and Towing or Refueling have right-of-way over all other engine-driven aircraft.
Converging and Categories: Balloons > Gliders > Airships > Powered Parachutes / Weight-Shift-Control / Airplanes / Rotorcraft. If same category, aircraft to the other's right has the right-of-way.
Specific Maneuvers - Approaching Head-On: Both to the right. Overtaking: The aircraft being overtaken has the right-of-way. The overtaking pilot must alter course to the right. Landing: Aircraft on final approach or in the act of landing have the right-of-way. When two or more aircraft are approaching to land, the lower altitude aircraft has the right-of-way.

question: Four forces of flight
answer_text: Lift, Weight, Thrust, Drag

question: How is Lift produced?
answer_text: Lift is produced by Bernoulli principle & Newton’s 3rd law

question: How does a pilot control lift?
answer_text: Change AOA (pitch), airspeed or flaps (technicaly also AOA)

question: What causes a stall?
answer_text: Exceeding critical angle of attack

question: What causes a spin?
answer_text: Uncoordinated aggravated stall resulting in a yawing, rolling, & pitching motion

question: How to recover from spin?
answer_text: PARE: Power Idle, Ailerons neutral, Rudder opposite to the direction of spin, Elevator forward

question: V-Speeds
answer_text:
VS0 (Stall speed in landing configuration): 49 MPH (43 KTS)
VS1 (Stall speed in clean configuration): 57 MPH (50 KTS)
VR (Rotation speed): 60 MPH (52 KTS)
VX (Best angle of climb): 65 MPH (56 KTS)
VY (Best rate of climb): 85 MPH (74 KTS)
VG (Best glide speed): 80 MPH (70 KTS)
VFE (Maximum flap extended speed): 100 MPH (87 KTS)
VA (Maneuvering speed at 2,300 lbs): 112 MPH (97 KTS)
VNO (Maximum structural cruising speed): 145 MPH (126 KTS)
VNE (Never exceed speed): 182 MPH (158 KTS)

question: High-voltage warning light/Ammeter discharge indicates
answer_text: Loss of alternator output, Malfunction of an Alternator, Belt broke, Switch on, CB popped, Faulty indication (bad light), RPM

question: Brake system type
answer_text: Hydraulic. Fluid color Red / pink

question: Vacuum system powers which instruments?
answer_text: Attitude Indicator & Heading Indicator

question: Turn coordinator power source
answer_text: Electrically driven

question: Airspeed indicator test
answer_text: 'Airspeed alive' while rolling on takeoff

question: Airspeed indicator works by
answer_text: Diaphragm compares the ram air against static pressure

question: Altimeter works by
answer_text: Aneroid wafer expands or contracts against static pressure

question: VSI works by
answer_text: Calibrated leak to equalize after leveling off

question: Fuel type
answer_text: 100LL

question: You’re in the practice area and your engine starts running rough, what could be the cause?
answer_text: Engine needs 3 things to function: magnetos, air, fuel. Magnetos: make sure keys are on 'both'. Air: Turn on carb heat (also alternate source of air for the engine). Fuel: Mixture rich & fuel selector set to both.

question: C172 engine description
answer_text: LHAND: Lycoming, Horizontally opposed, Air-cooled, Normally aspirated, Direct drive. 150 HP at 2700 RPM

question: Engine configuration
answer_text: 4 cylinders, 2 spark plugs each

question: What preflight action is required of a pilot prior to a flight?
answer_text: NWKRAFT: Notams available, Weather, Known ATC delays, Runway lengths available, Alternates, Fuel requirements and reserves, Take off and landing distance required, Obtain all information applicable to that flight

question: What endorsements are required for solo flight? What three documents must you have in your possession to solo an aircraft as a student?
answer_text: Logbook with endorsements: Pre-solo knowledge test 61.87(b), Make and Model proficiency 61.87(c), 90 days currency 61.87(n). Documents in possession: Student pilot license, Medical exam, Gov issued doc with photo, Logbook with endorsements

question: What type of medical do you need? How long is it valid?
answer_text: At least class 3, valid for 60 calendar months

question: What are the 4 turning tendencies?
answer_text: Gyroscopic precession: Spinning propeller creates gyroscopic effect. Any force that deflects its plane of rotation, results in a force manifested 90° in the direction of rotation. P-Factor: downward blade on the left has a higher aoa, thus generating more lift pushing aircraft to this side. Spiraling slipstream: wind stream hitting the vertical stabilizer on the left-hand side. Torque: based on Newton’s Third Law, as the engine and propeller revolve clockwise (from the pilot's view), an equal force tries to rotate the aircraft in the opposite direction, creating a roll to the left.

question: Traffic Pattern Legs
answer_text: Departure: Flight path starting at takeoff, continuing straight ahead until at least 1/2 mile beyond the runway end. Upwind: An extension of the departure leg along the extended centerline. Crosswind: Flight path at a right angle to the runway off its takeoff end. Downwind: A path parallel to the runway in the direction opposite to landing. Base: A path at a right angle to the landing runway off its approach end. Final: The final flight path aligned with the runway centerline extending to the touchdown point.

question: Radio Calls (Nontowered/CTAF)
answer_text: Outbound: Before taxiing and before taxiing on the runway for departure. Last call when leaving pattern. Inbound: 10 miles out: State identification, altitude, and intentions (e.g., 'landing'). Entering the pattern: Call when joining the downwind. Pattern legs: Call when on base and final. Exiting: Call when clear of the runway.

question: What is the procedure for a Go-around?
answer_text: 4 C’s. Cram: throttle + carb heat in. Climb: Vx or Vy as appropriate (500’). Clean: remove flaps after positive rate of climb. Communicate: going around.

question: What is the procedure for steep turns?
answer_text: 4C’s and E. Start at a cardinal heading if possible, At VA or below (100 MPH), Power to maintain altitude (Carb heat and trim if needed), Step into the turn with left rudder with 30* bank, Add power (200 RPM, carb heat off if needed), Nose up trim (2 full swipes), Bank to 45*, Complete 360* (Increase/decrease backpressure for altitude), Roll out 15* beforehand, Keep configuration, remove rudder and apply forward pressure, Step into the right turn with right rudder, Bank to 45*, Add trim (1/2 swipe), Roll out 15* beforehand.

question: How to recover from steep turn?
answer_text: Remove right rudder, Forward pressure to maintain altitude, Back to cruise setting (remove carb heat if on)

question: The definition of careless or reckless operation.
answer_text: FAR 91.13: Aircraft operation other than for the purpose of air navigation. So as to endanger the life or property of another.

question: What is the authority and responsibility of the pilot in command?
answer_text: FAR 91.3: The pilot in command of an aircraft is directly responsible for, and final authority as to the operation of the aircraft. In an in-flight emergency, may deviate from any rule to meet that emergency. If deviation happens, upon the request of the administrator, send a written report of that deviation.

question: What would happen to the altimeter if the static port is blocked?
answer_text: It freezes the altimeter at the altitude where it became blocked

question: What would happen to the VSI if the static port is blocked?
answer_text: Vertical speed indicator (VSI) remains at zero

question: What would happen to the airspeed indicator if the static port is blocked?
answer_text: Provide inaccurate readings: Climb – much slower, Descent – much faster

question: How to handle a problem with the static port?
answer_text: Alternative static port. Due to lower pressure in the cabin, consider: Altitude: +50’, VSI: Momentary climb, Airspeed: +11 Knots

question: What is the procedure if you are lost?
answer_text: 5 C’s: Climb, Circle, Confess, Comply, Conserve

question: What are the types of fog?
answer_text: PRAFUS. Precipitation: warmer rain falls through cool air. Radiation: Forms on clear, calm nights as the ground cools the surrounding air. Advection: Coastal area, occurs when warm, moist air moves over a cold surface. Freezing: -25 °F or colder. When water vapor forms directly into ice crystals instead of droplets. Upslope: Results from moist, stable air being forced up sloping terrain. Steam: Forms when cold, dry air moves over warm water.

question: What are the types of ice?
answer_text: Rime: Rough, milky, and opaque; formed by instantaneous freezing of small, supercooled droplets. Clear: Glossy and translucent; formed by slow freezing of large, supercooled droplets. Mixed: A combination of rime and clear ice. Frost: Ice crystals formed by sublimation when both the temperature and dew point are below freezing.

question: What are the types of hypoxias?
answer_text: Air (Hypoxic): Insufficient oxygen in the lungs, typically caused by the reduced partial pressure of oxygen at high altitudes. Blood (Hypemic): The blood's inability to carry or transport oxygen, often due to carbon monoxide (CO) poisoning, anemia, or recent blood donation. Circulation (Stagnant): Oxygen-rich blood is not moving to the tissues that need it, caused by heart failure, shock, or excessive G-forces. Drugs (Histotoxic): Cells are unable to effectively use the oxygen delivered to them because they have been poisoned by toxins like alcohol or narcotics. Warning Signs: Look for cyanosis (blue fingernails/lips), euphoria, headache, impaired judgment, and drowsiness.

question: Describe each of the light gun signals available from ATC.
Which turn direction is standard for an airport traffic pattern?
answer_text: At untowered airport it is standard to circle to the left unless otherwise advised in the chart supplement.

question: When are you permitted to deviate from an ATC instruction?
answer_text:
FAR 91.123
- Amended clearance is obtained
- During emergency situations
- In response to TCAS (Traffic alert and Collision Avoidance System)

question: Can a student pilot fly into Class B airspace? If so, what is required? What about ORD?
answer_text:
FAR 61.95:
A student may not operate in class B airspace unless:
-	Specific ground and flight training is provided by an authorized instructor
-	The student’s logbook has an endorsement, dated within 90 days, specifying that training was given and student is proficient.
FAR 91, appendix D, section 4: ORD is included in the list where solo students are not allowed.

question: What is the minimum reserve fuel required for day and night VFR operations?
answer_text:
VFR day: first intended point + 30 minutes
VFR night: first intended point + 45 minutes

question: What is a stall? How do I recover?
answer_text: AC No: 61-67C - Stall is the total loss of lift due to the interruption of smooth airflow in the surface of the wings. To recover, pitch forward, add full power, return the airplane to desired conditions and stablish a coordinated straight and level flight.

question: What are two types of drag? Describe them.
answer_text:
Induced and parasite drag.
Induced drag is the byproduct of lift in the form of wingtip vortices.
Parasite drag is all forces to slow the aircraft linked to its shape and surface that are not associated with generating lift.

question: Describe the details regarding and AD? Are they required?
answer_text: Airworthiness Directive (AD) are regulatory notices issued by the FAA to require the correction of unsafe conditions of aircrafts, engines, propellers and appliances. They are mandatory and must be complied with.

question: Describe the details regarding a SB. Are they required?
answer_text: Service bulletins are notices issued by the aircraft or component manufacturer to inform its owner or operator of recommended maintenance, inspection or design changes to improve safety, performance or reliability.

question: Describe how to determine if you, the pilot, is airworthy?
answer_text:
- License
- Medical
- Gov issued document with photo
- Logbook (for solo)
- Biannual flight review
- To take passengers: Passenger currency, 3 takeoff and landings in previous 90 days. For night time, 3 takeoff and landings 1hour after sunset and 1 hour before sunrise with full stop.

question: List the procedure to respond to an engine fire on the ground while starting.
answer_text:
-	Continue cranking engine
-	If Start – Run Engine 1700 RPM a few minutes, shutdown then inspect
-	If no start – Mixture cutoff and fuel selector off
-	Throttle – Full opem
-	Continue cranking engine 2-3 minutes
-	Fuel Selector – Off

question: List the procedure for the loss of communication radio when arriving at an airport with an operating control tower.
answer_text:
-	Check circuit breakers & volume
-	Check headset plugs
-	Check headset volume
-	Alternator switch – Recycle
-	Squawk 7600
-	Check freq.
-	Confirm with known freq.
